drupalNodeEvent.title: In-Person Event | News Media in an Authoritarian Age

field_speakers.processed:
The panelists are: 
Jonathan Derbyshire
US Opinion Editor, Financial Times
Jonathan Derbyshire is the Financial Times’ (FT) US opinion editor. He was previously Managing Editor of Prospect, Britain’s leading monthly magazine of politics and ideas, and Culture Editor of the New Statesman. Jonathan has also written for a number of other publications, including the Guardian, the Observer and the Times Literary Supplement. In a previous life, he taught philosophy in several British universities. 

Sipan Li
Visiting Scholar, Stanford University
Li Jun (also known as Li Sipan, 李思磐), Ph.D., is a visiting scholar at Stanford University, a scholar-in-residence at the University of California, Berkeley, and an associate professor at the Cheung Kong School of Journalism and Communication at Shantou University in China. A journalist, scholar, and feminist advocate, she has written extensively on public discourse, China’s feminist movement, the development of women’s NGOs, and social media activism. Her doctoral research, “Social Movements, Media, and the State: The New Feminist Movement with Communication as Core in Contemporary China (2003–2016),” examines these dynamics in depth.  

Rollo Romig
Pulitzer Prize Finalist, Journalist, Critic, Essayist 
Born and raised in Detroit, Rollo Romig is a journalist, essayist, critic, and the author of Pulitzer Prize finalist I Am on the Hit List: A Journalist’s Murder and the Rise of Autocracy in India. He has been reporting on South India since 2013, most often for The New York Times Magazine. His book I Am on the Hit List is a gripping investigation into the mysterious assassination of a journalist in India, revealing the courage and vulnerability of those who are fighting the decline of democracy around the world.

drupalNodeEvent.field_event_venue: Location: A407, 4th Floor A Johnson/Kaplan  66 West 12th Street New York City - 10003

field_description.processed: On Thursday, November 13 from 5:00-6:30pm EST, please join us at The New School for an in-person  panel discussion on the shifting landscapes of news media in India, China, the United States and the United Kingdom. An authoritarian turn in countries around the world is eroding older norms of press freedom.  A varied playbook of intimidating journalists includes the use of laws on libel to sanction commercial print media outlets, withdrawal of funding, surveillance, trolling and physical attacks on journalists, and the criminalization of dissent under vague national security and defamation laws. Panelists will discuss how regimes of India, China, the United States and the UK have tried to restrict if not control what journalists and writers produce, as well as coping strategies that are being deployed in response.
